conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.

1/3
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for as many ZUP as possible. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources. I am willing to sell it for at least 40 ZUP to break even </my goals>
<reason> I need to sell the object for at least 40 ZUP to break even </reason>
<player answer> RED </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 35 | RED Receives ZUP: 35 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I am willing to sell the object for 35 ZUP. If you reject, I will propose a new trade with a higher price. </message>

iterations.feedback:
<critique>
1. Format: OK, but I could be more specific about the proposed trade. Instead of "Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 20", I could specify "Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 20 | Player RED Receives ZUP: 20" to clearly show the exchange. 
2. Payoff alignment: The proposed trade of 20 ZUP does not take into account the 40 ZUP production cost, so I am actually losing 20 ZUP in this trade. To advance my payoff, I should aim for at least 40 ZUP. A slightly different split, such as 30 ZUP, would still be acceptable and give me a higher payoff.
3. Opponent plausibility: Without any information about Player BLUE's resources or willingness to pay, it's hard to say whether this proposal will be accepted. However, since I have only 1 X and Player BLUE has only given ZUP, it's likely that this proposal will be accepted.
4. Consistency: My <reason> and <player answer> are consistent with the proposal, but my <message> implies that I am open to negotiating, which may lead Player BLUE to think that I am willing to accept a lower price.
5. Rule compliance: I am within my 3-proposal limit, so I am compliant with the rules.
</critique>

Instead of saying "If you reject, I will propose a new trade with a higher price or reject the trade if it is not possible to reach a mutually beneficial agreement", I could simply say "If you reject, I will propose a new trade with a higher price".
2. Payoff alignment: The proposed trade of 30 ZUP still does not take into account the 40 ZUP production cost, so I am actually losing 10 ZUP in this trade. To advance my payoff, I should aim for at least 40 ZUP. A slightly different split, such as 35 ZUP, would still be acceptable and give me a higher payoff.
3. Opponent plausibility: The previous proposal of 20 ZUP was rejected, and now I am proposing 30 ZUP. It's likely that Player BLUE will reject this proposal as well, as it's still below the break-even point. I should consider proposing a trade that is more likely to be accepted.
4. Consistency: My <reason> and <player answer> are consistent with the proposal, but my <message> implies that I am open to negotiating, which may lead Player BLUE to think that I am willing to accept a lower price.
5. Rule compliance: I am within my 3-proposal limit, so I am compliant with the rules.
</critique>
